content.text: Linux processes are organized into a global process tree that the kernel maintains. A PID namespace partitions this tree so that a group of processes has its own isolated view of process IDs, starting over from PID 1. This mechanism is one of the core building blocks that makes Linux containers possible: every Docker container runs inside its own PID namespace, giving it an independent init process while remaining fully supervised by the host kernel.

A PID namespace is a kernel abstraction that creates an isolated process ID space. Processes inside the namespace see a private counter that starts at 1, independent of the PIDs assigned to those same processes in the host or parent namespace. The kernel maintains a translation table that maps each process's in-namespace PID to its global PID, and every process can have a different PID in each namespace it participates in.

PID namespaces are hierarchical. A process in a parent namespace can see and signal processes in all child namespaces using the child's global PID. Processes in a child namespace, however, cannot see anything in the parent namespace — they see only the processes that share their namespace or that live in namespaces nested beneath them. This asymmetry is deliberate: it ensures container processes are isolated from each other and from the host while the host retains full administrative visibility.
The Linux kernel introduced PID namespaces in version 3.8 (2013) as part of the broader namespace subsystem. They are created using the
command. Container runtimes such as Docker, Podman, and containerd create a PID namespace automatically for every container.

Without PID namespace isolation, every process on a Linux system shares the same global process tree. That means a process running inside a container could enumerate all other processes on the host, discover PIDs belonging to other containers or privileged system services, and potentially send signals to them. This is a significant security and operational problem in multi-tenant environments.

PID namespaces solve this by giving each container its own process tree. The container's root process starts as PID 1 — the init position — so the container feels like a complete operating system even though it is just a group of processes on the host. PID 1 has special semantics: when it exits or is killed, the kernel automatically sends
to all other processes in the namespace, cleanly reaping the entire container in one step.

PID namespace isolation also enables multiple independent software stacks to coexist on the same machine without interference. Two containers can each run a database, a web server, or any service — each seeing its own PID 1, its own workers at PID 2, 3, and so on — without any awareness of the other. This is foundational to how Kubernetes schedules pods and how cloud providers run multi-tenant workloads.
Container processes cannot enumerate or signal host processes or processes in sibling namespaces. Leaking process information across tenants is structurally prevented.
Every container gets a PID 1. Software that relies on being the init process (signal reaping, zombie collection, graceful shutdown) works exactly as it would on a standalone machine.
When PID 1 of a namespace exits, the kernel terminates the entire namespace and all processes in it. There are no orphan processes to clean up manually.
Applications that hard-code assumptions about PID numbers or rely on
work correctly because they see a private and coherent process tree.
PID namespaces can be nested, enabling container-in-container scenarios (e.g., Kubernetes DinD — Docker in Docker) where each layer has its own isolated process tree.
The host always sees all processes under their host PIDs, enabling
, and system-level monitoring to work on all containers without entering each namespace.
The process that becomes PID 1 must correctly handle zombie reaping and signal forwarding. A naive application binary (not a real init) as PID 1 will accumulate zombie processes and ignore
. This is a common source of container shutdown problems.
Joining an existing PID namespace requires that the joining process set
tool handles this transparently, but it adds operational complexity when debugging live containers.
PID namespace isolation does not automatically filter
inside the namespace, processes can still read the host's
and discover host PIDs. A container runtime must mount a new
inside a container and sees PID 42, the host's
must use the host PID (e.g., 9321). Correlating in-container PIDs to host PIDs requires extra tooling.
PID namespace cannot be unshared after fork.

A process can create a new PID namespace for its children but cannot move itself into a new PID namespace after it has already forked. The namespace assignment is permanent for the process's lifetime.
At the kernel level, each process holds a reference to the PID namespace it belongs to via the
structure holds a pointer to a

object that tracks all active PIDs inside that namespace. When the kernel assigns a new PID, it increments the namespace's local counter and also records a PID in every ancestor namespace. The resulting data structure is a chain: the process has one PID per namespace level it occupies.
When a new process is created inside a PID namespace via
Allocates a PID entry in the child namespace (e.g., PID 2 from the container's perspective).
Allocates a PID entry in every ancestor namespace up to the root (e.g., PID 9322 in the host namespace).
Stores both PIDs in the process's struct, linked to their respective namespace objects.
returns the PID as seen from the calling process's own namespace — it always returns the in-namespace PID.
filesystem is namespace-aware. When a new
is mounted inside a PID namespace, it only exposes directories for processes that are visible in that namespace.
refers to the container's init process. Running
and reports only in-namespace PIDs, giving the appearance of an isolated system.
, a process in a new PID namespace would still see the host's
containing all host process directories. Docker automatically bind-mounts a new
for each container via its OCI runtime configuration.
PID 1 in a namespace inherits the special behavior that Linux defines for the global init process:

sent to PID 1 from within the namespace are ignored unless PID 1 has explicitly installed a signal handler for them. This mirrors the behavior of the global init process, which cannot be killed by ordinary signals. Signals sent from
the namespace (e.g., from the host) are not suppressed —
to the container's PID 1 using its host-namespace PID.

The diagram below shows a host running two containers. The host's PID namespace sees all processes by their global PIDs (47, 83, 84, 85, …). Each container has its own PID namespace where the root process is always PID 1. The kernel maintains a translation table between in-namespace PIDs and global PIDs.

attrs.alt: PID Namespace Architecture: host namespace sees all PIDs; each container has its own isolated PID tree starting at PID 1

content.text: The dashed lines show the PID mapping: nginx running as PID 1 inside Container A is simultaneously PID 47 in the host namespace. Similarly, postgres is PID 1 inside Container B but PID 83 on the host. Both containers are invisible to each other — they cannot enumerate or signal the other's processes.

PID namespaces are used automatically by every major container runtime — there is rarely a need to create one manually unless building a custom container runtime, a system testing harness, or a sandboxing framework. Understanding when PID namespace isolation is appropriate (or inappropriate) matters most when deciding how to configure it:
Whenever running untrusted or multi-tenant workloads. Isolated PID namespaces prevent information disclosure and cross-container signal injection.
When an application expects to be PID 1 — particularly init-style service managers or supervisors that must reap orphan processes.
When a container needs to monitor, debug, or interact with host processes directly — for example, a performance profiler that must attach to arbitrary host PIDs, or a privileged monitoring sidecar.
When two containers must share a process namespace — for example, a sidecar that instruments the main application via
Creating a PID Namespace with unshare
command creates a new PID namespace and runs a given command inside it. The
flag is required because the PID namespace takes effect for children of the calling process, not the process itself.

# Create a new PID namespace and run bash inside it
# --pid: create new PID namespace
# --fork: fork before exec so the child becomes PID 1
# --mount-proc: mount a new /proc filtered to the new namespace
sudo unshare --pid --fork --mount-proc bash

# Inside the new namespace:
echo $$          # prints 1  (this bash IS PID 1)
ps aux           # shows only bash (PID 1) and ps (PID 2)

# From a second terminal on the host, find the actual host PIDs:
ps aux | grep bash   # shows the real host PID, e.g. 14823
Using the clone(2) System Call Directly
Container runtimes create PID namespaces via the
flag. The child process automatically becomes PID 1 in the new namespace. The following C program demonstrates the mechanism:
Docker creates an isolated PID namespace for every container by default. The namespace mode can be changed with

# Default: isolated PID namespace (each container sees only its own processes)
docker run --rm alpine ps aux
# PID   USER     COMMAND
#   1   root     ps aux    ← only this process exists from inside

# Share the host PID namespace (container sees ALL host processes)
docker run --rm --pid=host alpine ps aux
# PID   USER     COMMAND
#   1   root     /sbin/init
#   2   root     [kthreadd]
#   ...  (full host process list)

# Share namespace with another running container
docker run -d --name app nginx
docker run --rm --pid=container:app alpine ps aux
# Shows nginx processes with their in-app-namespace PIDs

# Terminal 1: start a container
docker run -it --rm --name demo alpine sh

# Inside the container:
ps aux
# PID   USER     COMMAND
#   1   root     sh
# Only one process visible — the shell itself

echo $$   # prints 1 — the shell is PID 1

# Terminal 2: check the host's view of the same process
docker inspect demo --format '{{.State.Pid}}'
# e.g., 12801  ← the host PID for the container's PID 1

ls /proc/12801/ns/pid
# /proc/12801/ns/pid -> pid:[4026532400]  ← namespace inode
Advanced: Entering a Running Container's PID Namespace

# Start a container running a background process
docker run -d --name web nginx

# Get the container's init PID on the host
CONTAINER_PID=$(docker inspect web --format '{{.State.Pid}}')
echo $CONTAINER_PID   # e.g. 9403

# Enter the container's PID namespace from the host (without docker exec)
# nsenter uses /proc/<pid>/ns/pid to join the namespace
sudo nsenter --target $CONTAINER_PID --pid --mount bash

# Now inside the container's namespace:
ps aux
# PID   USER     COMMAND
#   1   root     nginx: master process
#   7   nginx    nginx: worker process
#  12   root     bash    ← this shell

# List namespace details
readlink /proc/self/ns/pid   # pid:[4026532201]  same as the container
readlink /proc/$CONTAINER_PID/ns/pid   # also pid:[4026532201]
Zombie Process Demo: PID 1 Must Reap Children

# Bad: direct bash as PID 1 does not reap zombies properly
docker run --rm alpine sh -c 'sleep 5 & wait'
# Works, but bash as PID 1 has subtle zombie reaping issues
# across complex signal chains

# Better: use an explicit init (tini)
docker run --rm --init alpine sh -c 'sleep 5 & wait'
# --init adds tini as PID 1; tini properly reaps all children
# and forwards signals

# Inspect what PID 1 is in each case:
docker run --rm alpine cat /proc/1/cmdline | tr '\0' ' '
# Output: sh -c ...   (bare shell as PID 1)

docker run --rm --init alpine cat /proc/1/cmdline | tr '\0' ' '
# Output: /sbin/tini -- sh -c ...  (tini as PID 1)
A production Kubernetes cluster running 50 pods on a single node illustrates PID namespace behavior at scale. Each pod gets its own PID namespace (configurable via
in the pod spec to share across containers in the pod). The node's kernel simultaneously tracks thousands of PIDs across dozens of namespaces.

# Kubernetes pod spec with shared PID namespace
# (useful when a sidecar container needs to inspect the main container's processes)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: app-with-debugger
spec:
  shareProcessNamespace: true   # both containers share one PID namespace
  containers:
    - name: app
      image: myapp:latest

    - name: debugger
      image: busybox
      command: ["sh", "-c", "while true; do ps aux; sleep 10; done"]
      # This container can see app's processes because namespace is shared

(the default), each container in a pod has its own namespace. This means PID 1 in each container is independent, and a crash in one container's init process does not cascade to sibling containers. The Kubernetes kubelet monitors the pod via
and the container runtime, using host-namespace PIDs that are visible in
on the node, regardless of namespace configuration.
A common production scenario is running a Java application alongside a Prometheus JVM agent. Without
, the agent sidecar cannot attach to the Java process because

requires a shared PID namespace. Enabling the shared namespace allows the agent to attach to the Java process by its in-namespace PID while maintaining full network and filesystem isolation between the two containers.
Running an Application Directly as PID 1

# ❌ BAD: node process becomes PID 1 without zombie reaping
FROM node:20-alpine
COPY app.js .
CMD ["node", "app.js"]
# node does not install a SIGCHLD handler for zombie reaping.
# If node spawns child processes (e.g., via child_process.spawn),
# their zombies accumulate in /proc indefinitely.

# ✅ GOOD: add --init to let tini handle PID 1 responsibilities
# Option A: docker run --init flag
# docker run --init myimage

# Option B: embed tini in the Dockerfile
FROM node:20-alpine
RUN apk add --no-cache tini
COPY app.js .
ENTRYPOINT ["/sbin/tini", "--"]
CMD ["node", "app.js"]
# tini becomes PID 1, reaps zombies, forwards SIGTERM

# ❌ BAD: sharing the host PID namespace for a regular application
docker run --pid=host myapp
# The container can now see and signal ALL host processes.
# A bug or exploit in the container could kill critical host services.

# ✅ GOOD: only use --pid=host when the container genuinely needs it
# (e.g., a system profiler like nsenter or perf)
docker run --pid=host --rm --cap-add SYS_PTRACE \
  ghcr.io/nicholasgasior/gdb-alpine gdb -p $HOST_PID

# ❌ BAD: create PID namespace without --mount-proc
sudo unshare --pid --fork bash
ps aux
# Shows ALL host processes — /proc still shows the host namespace!

# ✅ GOOD: always add --mount-proc when creating a PID namespace manually
sudo unshare --pid --fork --mount-proc bash
ps aux
# Shows only the processes in the new namespace
Killing PID 1 from Inside the Namespace

# Inside a container, signals to PID 1 from within the namespace are ignored
# unless PID 1 has set up a handler:
kill -TERM 1   # no effect if PID 1 does not handle SIGTERM internally
kill -KILL 1   # SIGKILL to PID 1 from within the namespace is also suppressed

# ✅ To stop a container, signal from the HOST using the host PID:
docker stop mycontainer
# docker sends SIGTERM to the container's host PID, which bypasses
# the namespace's signal suppression
Always use a proper init process as PID 1.
flag for any container that spawns child processes. This ensures zombie reaping and proper signal propagation to the application.
Regardless of what is PID 1, the application that receives a shutdown signal must handle
gracefully. If running without a dedicated init, install an explicit signal handler in the application. The default Docker stop timeout is 10 seconds before
Sharing the host PID namespace is a privilege escalation risk. It is appropriate only for trusted debugging or profiling tools, and should be combined with other security controls (
Use nsenter for live debugging instead of docker exec.
enters the container's namespaces with a fresh shell. This is useful when the container image lacks a shell (distroless images), because the tool binary runs from the host filesystem.
A container that accumulates zombie processes is a sign that PID 1 is not reaping. Expose a metric or alert on
PID namespace creation has negligible overhead. The kernel data structures involved — a

struct and a per-namespace PID table — are small and allocated from a kernel slab allocator. Benchmarks measuring container startup latency consistently show that PID namespace creation is not a bottleneck compared to image layer mounting or cgroup setup.

call level, the overhead of namespace translation is a single pointer dereference into the namespace table — essentially free. There is no measurable syscall overhead difference between a process in a namespace and one in the root namespace.
The main scalability consideration is the
for each container is inexpensive individually, but on nodes running 200+ pods, the number of
mount points grows proportionally. This is managed by the container runtime (containerd, cri-o) and is accounted for in kernel memory usage via the slab allocator, but it is worth tracking
The maximum number of PIDs in a namespace is limited by

(default 32768 on 32-bit systems, 4194304 on 64-bit). This limit applies per-namespace, so 500 containers each with 100 processes consume 50,000 total host PIDs — well under the 64-bit limit in practice.
Part of util-linux. Creates new namespaces from the command line. Essential for manual namespace experimentation.
Enters an existing namespace identified by a file descriptor or a path in
internally and directly by sysadmins for distroless container debugging.
Lists all namespaces on the system with their type, inode, and associated process. Useful for auditing what namespaces exist:

# List all PID namespaces on the system
lsns --type pid
# NS          TYPE  NPROCS  PID USER  COMMAND
# 4026531836  pid      142    1 root  /sbin/init
# 4026532201  pid        3 9403 root  nginx: master process
# 4026532300  pid        2 9821 root  postgres

# Inspect a container's namespace inode
docker inspect mycontainer --format '{{.State.Pid}}' | xargs -I{} ls -la /proc/{}/ns/pid
# lrwxrwxrwx ... /proc/9403/ns/pid -> pid:[4026532201]
Minimal init specifically designed for containers. Reaps zombie processes and forwards signals correctly. Bundled in many base images and enabled with
Similar to tini, by Yelp. Available as a Debian/Alpine package. Used as ENTRYPOINT in Dockerfiles.
Tools that translate between host PIDs and container PIDs for debugging live systems without entering the container.
to enable PID namespace isolation. Docker, Podman, and containerd all use runc (or a compatible shim) under the hood.
Separate PID counter per group; full hierarchy
Host sees all via host PIDs
Resource limits only; no PID visibility isolation
Full kernel isolation; separate process tree per VM
Host sees only the VM process
Strong multi-tenant isolation, legacy OS support
PID namespaces contribute significantly to container security, but they are not a complete sandbox on their own. Several attack surface considerations apply:
on any process it can see. If PID namespace isolation is combined with
or the process has host network access, this can be exploited to read or inject into host processes. Always apply

# ❌ Privileged mode disables PID namespace isolation effectively
docker run --privileged myimage
# The container has CAP_SYS_ADMIN and can remount /proc, create new
# namespaces, and see/kill host processes.

# ✅ Drop all capabilities and add only what is needed
docker run \
  --cap-drop ALL \
  --cap-add NET_BIND_SERVICE \
  --security-opt seccomp=default.json \
  myimage
# Even if PID namespace is set to host for some reason, the container
# cannot ptrace or kill without CAP_SYS_PTRACE and CAP_KILL
If PID 1 does not reap zombie processes, they accumulate in
and consume kernel process table entries. On a node with a tight
, zombie accumulation across many containers can eventually exhaust the PID table, causing
to fail with ENOMEM system-wide. Monitoring
vs active PID counts is recommended for high-density container nodes.

Think of PID namespaces like hotel room numbering. From the perspective of a guest in room 101, that is their room — it is Room 1 in their private world if they happen to be the only tenant who thinks about room numbers abstractly. But the hotel management (the host kernel) has a global floor plan where that same room is uniquely identified by its real room number (e.g., floor 3, room 301 in the global system). Two guests in different rooms can both call their room "Room 101" without any conflict, because their references are scoped to their own namespaces. Hotel staff (the kernel) always use the real, global room number when moving between rooms or managing the building.

The other key insight is the asymmetry: guests cannot see into other guests' rooms (containers cannot see each other's processes), but hotel staff can open any door (the host can inspect and signal any container process). This asymmetry is not a limitation — it is the intended security model.
A PID namespace gives a group of processes its own process ID counter, starting at PID 1, isolated from all other namespaces on the same kernel.
Every Docker container gets an isolated PID namespace by default. The container's root process appears as PID 1 inside the namespace and as a unique host PID outside it.
PID namespace isolation is asymmetric: the host can see and signal all container processes via their host PIDs; container processes cannot see the host or other containers.
must be remounted inside the namespace (via a new
) to filter the visible process list. Without this,
still exposes the full host process tree.
When PID 1 of a namespace exits, the kernel kills every other process in that namespace — this is the mechanism behind clean container shutdown.
Always use a real init process (tini, dumb-init, or
) as PID 1 in containers that spawn child processes, to prevent zombie accumulation and ensure correct signal forwarding.
PID namespace creation has negligible performance overhead. The primary scalability concern at high pod density is
mount proliferation and kernel PID table memory, both of which are well-managed by modern container runtimes.
for live debugging. These tools allow entering a container's namespace without requiring a shell inside the container image.
